captions.01.png: Painting and siding repair on historic East Austin home
captions.02.png: Both sides of this back porch wall required a wood restorer to halt the deterioration and a filler sculpted to match the original woodwork
captions.03.png: The completed restoration of the wall after painting.
captions.04.png: The front of the house needed pressure washing, scraping, sanding, caulkingand repainting. The original paint was peeling and the caulking was deteriorating.
captions.05.png: The completed facade of the house after painting.
captions.06.jpeg: This Hyde Park home needed a new bathroom window for ventilation and code compliance. The walls of these old houses were built with studs that are actually 2 inchs by 4 inches. The inside sill required some custom trim work to transition into the shower stall.
captions.07.jpeg: The original trim was preserved and the owner opted to paint it himself afterwards to keep the cost down
captions.08.jpeg: The original back door was drafty and the owner wanted to replace it with a more energy efficient door that he had found at Habitat for Humanity.
captions.09.jpeg: This back wall wasn't remotely plumb but doors need to be to function properly. I used a trick i learned form an old home builder and cut some long thin triangles out 1 x 4s to accommodate for the gaps between the jamb and the trim. You can see some this in the next photo on the right side of the door.
captions.10.jpeg: I also filled this gap in the floor with foam and reused part of the original threshold to maintain the look. The owners were very happy with the results and their dog appreciates the new view out the back door.
